text: Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) have rarely been investigated as porous materials for electronic device applications due to their scarcity and lack of electric conductivity. In this work, we show that the newly-emerging family of conductive two-dimensional (2D) MOFs could be a novel class of chemiresistive materials for the detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). The sensor array based on a small set of structurally similar yet chemically diverse 2D MOFs is capable of successfully and selectively distinguishing and identifying multiple categories of VOCs with great accuracy. Our findings indicate that the newly-fabricated materials detect the presence of different VOC molecules via multiple orthogonal sensing routes, such as charge transfer that is unique to this family of MOF materials, while excluding other surface interactions such as hydrogen and van der Waals forces commonly present in most chemiresistor-based sensors. Therefore, conductive MOFs could offer a flexible yet chemically distinctive sensing platform for potential use in future sensor devices.
